short_recommendation: Designate §4.2.4 / §4.2.5 (Part 4: Live Power Dashboard, Eclipse Section) as the canonical hub for the (baseline × coverage × geometry-weight) shared-arithmetic argument. ANALYST OVERRIDE of curmudgeon recommendation: curmudgeon recommended §2b.6 as hub (April 2026 Scoreboard Drift) because the W004 label originates in repository-encoded ensemble fits; analyst override rationale is that the magnitude-band non-discrimination is event-physics (the eclipse prediction formula and its inputs), not scoring-rule pathology (which is what §2b.6 documents). §4.2.5 already houses the canonical 'No dome geometry enters the prediction' statement and is the de-facto reference point cross-linked from §9.2 ('See Section 4.2 for the full analysis'). Apply two patches this cycle: (P0) +1 paragraph in §2b.6 designating §4.2.4/§4.2.5 as canonical hub (~140 words); (P1) trim §9.2 first paragraph by ~30 words by collapsing the redundant dual-baseline description and routing readers to §4.2.5. Defer the bulk consolidation work in §6.14, §8.16, §8b.3 to follow-up EXPs since each requires section-specific judgment about what to keep as scaffolding vs reduce to one-line cross-ref.
kernel_of_truth: The shared-arithmetic argument — that ΔZ = baseline × coverage × geometry_weight produces overlapping magnitude ranges between the dome's W004 recalibration and standard Chapman-Sq-dynamo ionospheric physics — is the strongest single argument in our review against the August 2026 eclipse as a discriminating test. It is genuinely persuasive, fundamentally correct, and quantitatively verifiable. A defender skimming the site, however, would reasonably claim we are dressing up one good argument as six, because the same arithmetic appears (with minor framing variation) in part2b, part4, part6, part8, part8b, and part9. Our argument is stronger when stated canonically once and cross-referenced elsewhere — exactly the curmudgeon's diagnosis.
why_it_doesnt_save_the_argument: Picking §4.2 rather than §2b.6 as the hub doesn't weaken the argument — both placements are defensible, but §4.2 is where the eclipse prediction is being analyzed on its own physical terms (the Besselian-element coverage values, the BOU 2017 baseline measurement, the FSF=tan(lat) reduction), so it is the natural home for the arithmetic. §2b.6 remains the canonical home for the SCORING pathologies (100% problem, W004 scoreboard split, pred=obs hardcoding) that share the W004 label but are about results.json scoring rules, not about whether the eclipse prediction can be discriminated from standard physics. The override preserves §2b.6's identity as a scoring-rule audit rather than diluting it with event-physics arithmetic.

Lets §2b.6 keep all its scoring-rule content untouched while signposting where the arithmetic argument lives.

proposed_integration_patches.insertion_marker: The four scoring anomalies documented above — scoreboard split, pred=obs hardcoding, null-vs-null confirmation, and detection-threshold exclusion — were properties of the scoring rules as expressed in the live JSON, not transient data-entry errors. Each anomaly was reproduced across independent fetches on 2026-04-09 and 2026-04-10. The removal of the endpoint does not retroactively change what the rules computed while they were live.
proposed_integration_patches.new_paragraph: Where the magnitude-band arithmetic argument lives canonically. Several other sections (§4.2.4/§4.2.5, §6.14, §8b.3, §9.2) examine a related but distinct question: whether the dome's August 2026 eclipse prediction can be discriminated from standard physics in the magnitude band. That argument turns on the shared arithmetic ΔZ = baseline × coverage × geometry_weight — both the dome's W004 recalibration and the standard Chapman-Sq-dynamo mechanism produce overlapping ranges through that product. The arithmetic argument is canonical to §4.2.4 and §4.2.5 (Live Power Dashboard, Eclipse Test) because it concerns the eclipse prediction formula on its own physical terms — Besselian-element coverage, the BOU 2017 baseline measurement, the FSF=tan(lat) reduction. Section 2b.6 here documents the orthogonal SCORING pathologies (the 100% problem, the W004 scoreboard split, pred=obs hardcoding, detection-threshold exclusion) — these are about results.json scoring rules, not about whether the August 2026 prediction is discriminating. Sections elsewhere on the site that touch the magnitude-band arithmetic should cross-reference §4.2.5; sections that touch the W004 scoreboard split as a scoring-rule issue should reference §2b.6.

proposed_integration_patches.anchor_id: 9.2 The Eclipse Test: Not What It Appears
proposed_integration_patches.original_text_to_replace: The August 12, 2026 Eclipse Test is presented as the single most important discriminating prediction. The dome registers 9 per-station predictions across two baselines (BOU at −10.9 nT and W004 at −22.24 nT), producing a combined prediction range of approximately −5 to −26 nT with error bars. Four model-discriminating sub-tests (E-PRED-A through D) are registered, of which E-PRED-B (Hartland exceeds Ebro despite lower coverage) is the only one not trivially predicted by standard ionospheric physics, and we acknowledge it as a genuine discriminating sub-test: if Hartland’s anomaly exceeds Ebro’s in a pattern that cannot be explained by Hartland’s latitude-dependent Sq current geometry or local ionospheric conditions, this would be meaningful evidence for the dome’s FSF-from-H(r)/r claim. However, standard ionospheric factors (station latitude, E-region electron density, proximity to the Sq vortex focus) independently produce station-to-station variation, so E-PRED-B alone would not be conclusive. Setting that aside, the dual-baseline structure covers nearly the entire physically plausible quiet-day eclipse signal space — the Chapman ionospheric mechanism (peer-reviewed since 1933) predicts 5–25 nT under identical conditions, making the BOU baseline predictions entirely non-discriminating and the W004 baseline predictions overlapping at most stations. The W004 baseline itself was an "empirical recalibration" from a prior failed weekly test (v50.2), casting doubt on its independence. See Section 4.2 for the full analysis.
proposed_integration_patches.replacement_text: The August 12, 2026 Eclipse Test is presented as the single most important discriminating prediction. The dome registers 9 per-station predictions across two baselines (BOU at −10.9 nT and W004 at −22.24 nT). Four model-discriminating sub-tests (E-PRED-A through D) are registered; we acknowledge E-PRED-B (Hartland exceeds Ebro despite lower coverage) as a genuine discriminating sub-test in isolation — though standard ionospheric factors (latitude, E-region electron density, Sq vortex proximity) independently produce station-to-station variation, so it would not be conclusive alone. The dual-baseline structure covers nearly the entire physically plausible quiet-day signal space — Chapman ionospheric physics (peer-reviewed since 1933) predicts 5–25 nT under identical conditions — and the W004 baseline itself was an "empirical recalibration" from a prior failed weekly test (v50.2), casting doubt on its independence. See Section 4.2.4 and 4.2.5 for the full (baseline × coverage × geometry-weight) arithmetic.

anticipated_objections.objection: Curmudgeon's recommendation was §2b.6 as hub — why override?
anticipated_objections.response: Curmudgeon's case for §2b.6 is that the W004 label originates in the dome's repository-encoded ensemble fits, which §2b.6 documents. That is correct as far as labeling goes. But the SHARED ARITHMETIC argument is not about how the W004 label came to exist — it is about why ΔZ = baseline × coverage × geometry_weight cannot discriminate dome from standard physics. The latter is event-physics about the eclipse prediction formula. §4.2.4 already houses the four E-PRED tests and §4.2.5 the 'No dome geometry enters the prediction' canonical statement; the (baseline × coverage × FSF) arithmetic appears there in its fullest form. Putting the hub at §4.2 keeps §2b.6's identity as a scoring-rule audit, while §4.2 retains its identity as eclipse-prediction event-physics. This was a deliberate judgment call by the prior run and is preserved in this rescue authoring.

anticipated_objections.objection: Adding +140 words in §2b.6 to designate a hub elsewhere is itself bloat.
anticipated_objections.response: The 140-word designation is a one-time hub-creation cost. The downstream benefit is that future curmudgeon passes can flag W004/Chapman re-derivations as 'reduce to one-line cross-ref to §2b.6 → §4.2.5' rather than re-arguing whether each occurrence is duplicative. The §9.2 patch alone recovers 30 words; the deferred §6.14/§8b.3 work (separate EXPs) will recover more. Net trajectory is reduction once follow-up EXPs land.

anticipated_objections.objection: Why not also patch §6.14 / §8b.3 / §8.16 in this EXP?
anticipated_objections.response: Two reasons. First, §8b.3 is mid-integration via EXP-462 (Hole 1); re-counting after that lands gives an accurate post-restructure count. Second, §6.14's κ-cluster mentions need section-specific judgment about scaffolding vs redundancy, and bundling that here would make this EXP too large to integrate cleanly. Each deferred section can become its own follow-up if curmudgeon c3 still flags it.

anticipated_objections.objection: The §9.2 collapse loses information about the BOU baseline being 'entirely non-discriminating' and W004 'overlapping at most stations'.
anticipated_objections.response: Those phrases are restatements of the 5–25 nT vs −5 to −26 nT range overlap which the §4.2 cross-reference now carries. §9.2 keeps the high-level claim ('dual-baseline structure covers nearly the entire physically plausible quiet-day signal space') and the W004 'empirical recalibration' callout. The per-baseline breakdown is appropriate at §4.2's level of detail, not at §9.2's conclusion-summary level.

anticipated_objections.objection: Override of curmudgeon recommendation should require flagging for re-review.
anticipated_objections.response: Acknowledged. review_class=deep-attack is set deliberately so this EXP will be re-routed through curmudgeon-on-rewrite (PROP-038 Phase 1, class=deep-attack triggers Opus curmudgeon path) rather than verification-only. If curmudgeon disagrees with the §4.2 hub choice, the override can be reversed by a follow-up EXP that swaps the cross-pointer direction and moves the arithmetic statement from §4.2.5 to §2b.6 — no content is lost either way.
